Do this: Activate the correct virtual environment, run `python -m pip install <package>`, and verify `which python` and `python -m pip --version` point to the same environment.
Do this: Add characterization tests first, refactor in small verified steps, and keep behavior stable.
Do this: Add type hints to public APIs first, run a static checker, and gradually tighten strictness per module.
Do this: Always return or await promises and centralize error handling with `try/catch` around awaited boundaries.
Do this: Apply per-user and per-IP limits with burst and sustained windows to protect backend capacity.
Do this: Break circular imports by moving shared logic to a third module or importing lazily inside functions.
Do this: Cherry-pick focused commits when backporting fixes and reference original commit hashes for traceability.
Do this: Choose eventual consistency where latency matters, and reserve strong consistency for critical invariants.
Do this: Clean up timers and event listeners on unmount and avoid retaining stale closures.
Do this: Configure startup, readiness, and liveness probes separately so slow boot does not trigger false restarts.
Do this: Define SLOs around user outcomes, then alert on error budgets instead of raw CPU or memory thresholds.
Do this: Define freshness, completeness, and validity checks and fail fast when thresholds are breached.
Do this: Emit structured logs with stable keys so dashboards and alerts can aggregate reliably.
Do this: Gate deploys on tests and static checks, and make pipelines deterministic with pinned tool versions.
Do this: Index columns used in filters and joins, but avoid over-indexing high-write tables due to update overhead.
Do this: Invalidate based on ownership events and TTL fallback; avoid global cache clears during normal operations.
Do this: Keep JWT payload minimal, validate issuer/audience/expiry, and rotate signing keys safely.
Do this: Keep transactions short, access rows in consistent order, and implement safe retry logic.
Do this: Leverage `Cache-Control`, ETags, and validation requests to reduce latency and origin load.
Do this: Make migrations backward compatible, deploy code and schema safely, then remove deprecated fields later.
Do this: Memoize expensive calculations, keep component props stable, and avoid unnecessary parent re-renders.
Do this: Never mutate state directly; use immutable updates and functional `setState` patterns for derived changes.
Do this: Open the breaker on sustained failure rates and use half-open probes to restore traffic gradually.
Do this: Pick isolation based on anomaly tolerance; stronger levels reduce anomalies but can lower concurrency.
Do this: Prioritize correctness, security, and maintainability findings before style-level comments.
Do this: Propagate trace IDs through service boundaries and sample enough traffic to debug tail latency.
Do this: Publish machine-readable contracts and run consumer/provider contract tests in CI.
Do this: Rebase local feature commits onto updated main, resolve conflicts carefully, then push with `--force-with-lease`.
Do this: Set connect/read timeouts, implement retries with backoff on safe operations, and enforce overall request deadlines.
Do this: Standardize tooling, keep ownership boundaries clear, and optimize CI with affected-target execution.
Do this: Store secrets in a vault or cloud secret manager, rotate regularly, and never commit them to git.
Do this: Store timestamps in UTC, convert at presentation boundaries, and include timezone-aware parsing/formatting.
Do this: Test business rules and edge cases; keep mocks narrow and assert observable behavior over internals.
Do this: Test real component boundaries like DB plus API to catch contract mismatches early.
Do this: Tune alerts for actionability, document runbooks, and track toil reduction as a core metric.
Do this: Tune autovacuum and monitor table bloat to keep index and query performance stable over time.
Do this: Use `top`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, and `ss` together to separate CPU, memory, disk, and network bottlenecks.
Do this: Use `tracemalloc`, inspect object growth snapshots, and check long-lived global caches and reference cycles.
Do this: Use authorization code with PKCE for public clients and keep tokens off URLs and local storage when possible.
Do this: Use checkpointing and idempotent writes so retries recover safely without duplicate records.
Do this: Use compose profiles for optional services and mount source for rapid inner-loop feedback.
Do this: Use explicit TTLs, cache stampede protection, and cache invalidation keyed to data ownership boundaries.
Do this: Use fixtures for reusable setup and keep them small so tests remain explicit and understandable.
Do this: Use flags for controlled rollout and clean up stale flags quickly to avoid hidden complexity.
Do this: Use health-checked service registry entries and client-side retries with jitter for transient failures.
Do this: Use parameterized queries everywhere and never concatenate untrusted input into SQL text.
Do this: Use queues to smooth spikes, add retry policies with dead-letter handling, and track processing lag.
Do this: Use rolling or blue-green deployment with readiness checks before shifting traffic.
Do this: Use rolling updates with health gates and automatic rollback triggers based on error-rate SLOs.
Do this: Use same-site cookies plus CSRF tokens for state-changing browser requests.
Do this: Use semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, and sufficient contrast plus accessible labels.
Do this: Use threads for IO, processes for CPU-bound work, and async for high-concurrency network services.
Do this: Version features, document lineage, and enforce train-serving consistency checks.

Short guide: Add characterization tests first, refactor in small verified steps, and keep behavior stable.
Short guide: Add type hints to public APIs first, run a static checker, and gradually tighten strictness per module.
Short guide: Apply per-user and per-IP limits with burst and sustained windows to protect backend capacity.
Short guide: Code-split by route, lazy-load heavy dependencies, and audit bundle composition regularly.
Short guide: Define SLOs around user outcomes, then alert on error budgets instead of raw CPU or memory thresholds.
Short guide: Emit structured logs with stable keys so dashboards and alerts can aggregate reliably.
Short guide: Enable `strict`, fix `any` hotspots, and model edge cases with union types plus exhaustive narrowing.
Short guide: Enable slow query logs, rank by total latency, and optimize highest cumulative-cost queries first.
Short guide: Inspect execution plans, reduce row scans, and push predicates close to data sources.
Short guide: Invalidate based on ownership events and TTL fallback; avoid global cache clears during normal operations.
Short guide: Keep JWT payload minimal, validate issuer/audience/expiry, and rotate signing keys safely.
Short guide: Keep transactions short, access rows in consistent order, and implement safe retry logic.
Short guide: Leverage `Cache-Control`, ETags, and validation requests to reduce latency and origin load.
Short guide: Map domain errors to canonical status codes and include structured metadata for debuggability.
Short guide: Memoize expensive calculations, keep component props stable, and avoid unnecessary parent re-renders.
Short guide: Minimize round trips, compress payloads, and batch requests where it improves user-perceived speed.
Short guide: Never mutate state directly; use immutable updates and functional `setState` patterns for derived changes.
Short guide: Normalize text where required and test with multilingual and emoji inputs to avoid hidden parsing bugs.
Short guide: Open the breaker on sustained failure rates and use half-open probes to restore traffic gradually.
Short guide: Prefer `pyproject.toml`, pin runtime dependencies, and build reproducible lock files for deploys.
Short guide: Profile heap usage, stream large payloads, and cap concurrency to prevent burst-driven memory spikes.
Short guide: Propagate backpressure signals across boundaries and shed noncritical load before saturation.
Short guide: Propagate trace IDs through service boundaries and sample enough traffic to debug tail latency.
Short guide: Publish machine-readable contracts and run consumer/provider contract tests in CI.
Short guide: Remove timing assumptions, control randomness with seeds, and isolate shared mutable state.
Short guide: Resolve conflicts file-by-file, run tests immediately, and prefer small rebases to reduce merge complexity.
Short guide: Retry only idempotent operations by default and use exponential backoff with jitter.
Short guide: Set connect/read timeouts, implement retries with backoff on safe operations, and enforce overall request deadlines.
Short guide: Start with blast-radius reduction, then timeline reconstruction, then root-cause and verified fix rollout.
Short guide: Store secrets in a vault or cloud secret manager, rotate regularly, and never commit them to git.
Short guide: Store timestamps in UTC, convert at presentation boundaries, and include timezone-aware parsing/formatting.
Short guide: Test real component boundaries like DB plus API to catch contract mismatches early.
Short guide: Tune analyzers and ranking features with labeled queries, then evaluate offline and online metrics.
Short guide: Tune autovacuum and monitor table bloat to keep index and query performance stable over time.
Short guide: Use GIN indexes for containment queries on JSONB and avoid broad unselective index expressions.
Short guide: Use `@dataclass(frozen=True)` for immutable records and `default_factory` for mutable defaults.
Short guide: Use `asyncio` for IO-bound concurrency, avoid blocking calls inside coroutines, and wrap blocking work with executors.
Short guide: Use `top`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, and `ss` together to separate CPU, memory, disk, and network bottlenecks.
Short guide: Use authorization code with PKCE for public clients and keep tokens off URLs and local storage when possible.
Short guide: Use checkpointing and idempotent writes so retries recover safely without duplicate records.
Short guide: Use controlled inputs for validation-heavy forms and uncontrolled inputs for very large low-coupling forms.
Short guide: Use cursor pagination for large mutable datasets to avoid inconsistent page windows with offset pagination.
Short guide: Use explicit TTLs, cache stampede protection, and cache invalidation keyed to data ownership boundaries.
Short guide: Use flags for controlled rollout and clean up stale flags quickly to avoid hidden complexity.
Short guide: Use generics to preserve input-output type relationships instead of weakening APIs with broad types.
Short guide: Use health-checked service registry entries and client-side retries with jitter for transient failures.
Short guide: Use idempotency keys for create-like endpoints so client retries do not duplicate side effects.
Short guide: Use leases with expirations and fencing tokens; avoid indefinite locks without fail-safe expiration.
Short guide: Use queues to smooth spikes, add retry policies with dead-letter handling, and track processing lag.
Short guide: Use rolling or blue-green deployment with readiness checks before shifting traffic.
Short guide: Use slim base images, multi-stage builds, and only copy runtime artifacts into final images.
Short guide: Use structured logs, include request IDs, and avoid logging secrets or raw credentials.
Short guide: Use threads for IO, processes for CPU-bound work, and async for high-concurrency network services.
Short guide: Version features, document lineage, and enforce train-serving consistency checks.
Short guide: Version when breaking changes are unavoidable and publish migration notes with deprecation windows.
Short guide: Write blameless postmortems with clear actions, owners, and deadlines tied to measurable risk reduction.
